notes.anchor: I called in on the man we spoke of this morning
notes.note: A deliberately unnamed Caesarian insider (often identified with Caesar's intimate friend Gaius Matius) whom Cicero visited; the letter keeps him anonymous as a security precaution in the tense weeks after the assassination.

The Ides (the 13th or, in March, the 15th) of March 44 BC, the day Caesar was murdered; Cicero uses the date as shorthand for the assassination itself, the pivotal event of the whole post-Ides correspondence.
Marcus Aemilius Lepidus, Caesar's magister equitum (master of horse) in 44 BC, who held troops just outside Rome and would join Antony and Octavian in the Triumvirate of 43 BC.

notes.anchor: What a prudent man Oppius is
notes.note: Gaius Oppius, a Roman knight and one of Caesar's chief private agents (with Balbus); Cicero praises his discretion in mourning Caesar without giving offence to the assassins' side.

notes.anchor: whether what we hear about Sextus is firm enough
notes.note: Sextus Pompeius, the surviving younger son of Pompey the Great, who in 44 BC still commanded Republican forces in Spain and was a hoped-for rallying point against the Caesarians.

notes.anchor: when Brutus spoke at Nicaea on behalf of Deiotarus
notes.note: Deiotarus, king of Galatia, whom Brutus had defended before Caesar at Nicaea (in Bithynia); the anecdote shows Caesar's wary respect for Brutus's vehemence well before the conspiracy.
